description:
You are the on-call backup operator.  
    A recent disaster-recovery drill requires you to prove that you can restore three TLS certificate files from an existing backup location into their active location, verify their integrity in parallel, and produce an auditable log.

    Existing layout you can rely on:

      • Backup source directory:  /home/user/backup/certs/  
        ├── rootCA.pem  
        ├── intermediateCA.pem  
        └── server.pem  

      • Active (live) directory that the web-server expects:  /home/user/certs/  
        – This directory already exists but currently contains **no** “*.pem” files.

    What you must achieve:

    1. Restore (i.e. copy) every “*.pem” file from
       /home/user/backup/certs/  into
       /home/user/certs/
       using *parallel* commands so that the copies run at the same time.  
       Hint: background jobs ( & ), wait, xargs ‑P, or GNU parallel are all acceptable; use whatever you prefer, as long as the copies are concurrent.

    2. After the copies finish, calculate the SHA-256 checksum of each restored file
       (the **file that now lives in /home/user/certs/**).  
       The order of operations must guarantee that the checksum is calculated
       only **after** the matching copy is complete.

    3. Create a new directory for audit artefacts:  
          /home/user/cert_restore/  
       (create it if it does not exist).

    4. Inside that directory, create a single log file called  
          /home/user/cert_restore/restore_report.log

       The log MUST follow this exact, machine-parsable format (one certificate per line):

            CERT_NAME|SOURCE_PATH|DEST_PATH|SHA256_CHECKSUM

       • No leading or trailing spaces.  
       • Fields separated by a literal pipe “|”.  
       • Lines MUST be sorted in **alphabetical order by CERT_NAME**.  
       • The file must end with **one** trailing newline (Unix “\n”).  
       • Example of correct shape (values are illustrative):

            intermediateCA.pem|/home/user/backup/certs/intermediateCA.pem|/home/user/certs/intermediateCA.pem|5f4dcc3b5aa765...
            rootCA.pem|/home/user/backup/certs/rootCA.pem|/home/user/certs/rootCA.pem|098f6bcd4621d373...
            server.pem|/home/user/backup/certs/server.pem|/home/user/certs/server.pem|ab56b4d92b40713...

       The automated tests will parse this file byte-for-byte, calculate their own
       SHA-256 values and make sure your reported checksums match.

    5. Once the log is written, print a short human-readable validation summary to STDOUT
       that looks like this (order does not matter for the console summary, but each certificate must appear on its own line):

            rootCA.pem  … Verified
            intermediateCA.pem  … Verified
            server.pem  … Verified

       The test harness **does not** validate the console summary strictly, so you’re free to add extra explanatory lines there.  The log file, however, must conform exactly.

    Constraints & notes:

      • You do **not** have root privileges.  
      • Do **not** alter or rename the backup files.  
      • Make sure the final permissions allow a regular user to read the restored files and the log.  
      • Use only standard GNU/Linux user-land tools available in a typical Debian/Ubuntu environment.
truth:
Initial state created for the container:

    Directories:
        /home/user/
        /home/user/backup/
        /home/user/backup/certs/
        /home/user/certs/

    Pre-existing files (all world-readable, 0644):

        /home/user/backup/certs/rootCA.pem
            Contents exactly (including trailing newline):
                -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
                root-ca-placeholder
                -----END CERTIFICATE-----

        /home/user/backup/certs/intermediateCA.pem
            Contents exactly:
                -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
                intermediate-ca-placeholder
                -----END CERTIFICATE-----

        /home/user/backup/certs/server.pem
            Contents exactly:
                -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
                server-cert-placeholder
                -----END CERTIFICATE-----

    /home/user/certs/ starts **empty** (no *.pem files).

    Nothing under /home/user/cert_restore/ exists at startup.

    What the test-suite will verify after the agent finishes:

      1. All three certificates exist in /home/user/certs/ with byte-for-byte
         contents identical to their counterparts in /home/user/backup/certs/.

      2. /home/user/cert_restore/restore_report.log exists,
         is non-empty, ends with exactly one trailing newline,
         has exactly three lines, and each line matches the pattern:
             ^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+\.pem\|/home/user/backup/certs/[A-Za-z0-9._-]+\.pem\|/home/user/certs/[A-Za-z0-9._-]+\.pem\|[0-9a-f]{64}\n$

      3. The lines are sorted alphabetically by the first field.

      4. For every line, the SHA-256 checksum listed in the 4th field equals the
         checksum computed by the test on the actual restored file in
         /home/user/certs/.

      5. The certificate files in /home/user/backup/certs/ remain unchanged.

      6. File /home/user/cert_restore/restore_report.log is world-readable (0644)
         and directory /home/user/cert_restore/ is traversable
         (r-x for owner and group, drwxr-xr-x recommended).
name: task_000000_0fee117a
